coredata.dc:title: Intermolecular hydrogen bonding-mediated synthesis of high-quality photoluminescent carbon dots for label-free fluorometric detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Bisang
Li, Feiming
Zou, Liling
Chen, Dejian
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen bonding interactions guided supramolecular self-assembly has been widely studied, which played important roles in the field of nanotechnology and nanodevices. Herein, an effective intermolecular hydrogen bonding mediated heating-up synthesis of high-quality fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is developed based on hydrogen-bonding between trimesic acid and urea. Upon optimizing the hydrogen-bonding interaction, high quality of CDs with high monodispersity and stability could be obtained. Besides, these CDs could be used for sensitive and specific detection of ferric ions. The novel strategy may pave a new way in molecular engineering for the fabrication of high quality of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intermolecular hydrogen bonding
Carbon dots
Fe3+ ions detection
Fluorescence quenching
Electron transfer

Structural properties of the obtained CDs
Sensing performance of the CDs for the determination of Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of the TA-urea supramolecular system with different concentration ratios of TA/urea in (CD3)2SO at room temperature (a) and enlarge spectra (b, c).

(a) Emission wavelength plot of CDs prepared by different molor ratio of TA and urea. (b) UV–vsible absorption spectra (gray line) and photoluminescence spectra of CDs-23 aqueous solution under varying excitation wavelengths from 300 to 370 nm with 10 nm increments.

(a) TEM image showing the monodisperse nature of CDs-23, inset showing the corresponding size distribution of the CDs. (b) HRTEM image showing lattice fringes and interplanar spacing in CDs confirming the crystalline nature of the as-synthesized QDs. (c) The contrast intensity profile from the position marked with a red dashed line in (b). (d) SAED pattern of as-synthesized CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Effect of ionic strength, (b) pH values, and (c) irradiation times at 365 nm on photoluminescence emission intensity of CDs-23. (d) Photographs of CDs-23 in aqueous solution under UV irradiation for different times.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectrum of CDs-23 in the concentration of Fe3+ ions, inset showing visually detectable changes in the batches of bluish photoluminescence intensities of the CDs-23 treated Fe3+ ion, excited by UV light with wavelength of 365 nm in a UV chamber. (b) Nonlinear Stern-Volmer quenching profile of CDs-23 with Fe3+ ion concentration ranging between 0 and 1000 μM at pH = 6 (contact time = 1 min), inset showing linear part of the Stern-Volmer quenching profile with Fe3+ ion concentration ranging between 0 μM and 20 μM.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectrum of CDs-23 treated with interfering cations (1 mM) at pH = 6. (b) Bar diagram representing I0/I values of CDs-23 treated with respective interfering cations. (c) Photoluminescence emission spectrum of CDs-23 with interfering anions (1 mM). (d) Bar diagram representing I0/I values of CDs-23 treated with respective interfering anions.
Time-resolved photoluminescence kinetics (excitation at 397 nm) of CDs-23 aqueous solution in the absence (a) and presence (b) of Fe3+.
Schematic illustration of CDs synthesis from intermolecular hydrogen bonding guided self-assembly and low-temperature carbonization.
The proposed FL quenching mechanism for the C-dots/Fe3+ system.
Determination of Fe3+ in tap water samples using a CDs-23 nanoprobe.

head.ce:title: Intermolecular hydrogen bonding-mediated synthesis of high-quality photoluminescent carbon dots for label-free fluorometric detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry, Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry
Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intermolecular hydrogen bond-mediated synthesis of highly monodisperse crystalline and luminescent CDs with narrow size distributions.

Hydrogen bonding interactions guided supramolecular self-assembly has been widely studied, which played important roles in the field of nanotechnology and nanodevices. Herein, an effective intermolecular hydrogen bonding mediated heating-up synthesis of high-quality fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is developed based on hydrogen-bonding between trimesic acid and urea. Upon optimizing the hydrogen-bonding interaction, high quality of CDs with high monodispersity and stability could be obtained. Besides, these CDs could be used for sensitive and specific detection of ferric ions. The novel strategy may pave a new way in molecular engineering for the fabrication of high quality of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Noncovalent interactions guided supramolecular chemistry self-assembly including hydrogen bonding [1–4], nucleobase stacking [5], metal-ligand interactions [6], hydrophobic effects [7], and the others [8,9], have been served as powerful synthetic tools for fabricating higher order architectures, which is expected to be of great importance in the emerging field of nanotechnology and nanodevices [10,11]. In particular, hydrogen bonds mediating has been proved to be a promising strategy for controlling molecular self-assembly due to its reversibility, specificity, directionality, and cooperative strength of this class of noncovalent interactions [4,10]. Furthermore, hydrogen bonds mediating supramolecular architectures behave diverse natures, offering an immense scope for tailoring the physical, chemical, and stimuli-responsive nature of the materials resulted [12].

Carbon dots (CDs) were discovered over the past 10 years. Due to their remarkable optical and electrical properties, non-toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, CDs have been used in photovoltaic devices light-emitting diodes, photocatalytic water splitting, nano-diagnosis and sensors [13–17]. These outstanding application provoke people great interest to search for new synthesis methodologies for fabricating high quality of CDs. Until now, there are many methods have been developed. For instance, electrochemical oxidation processes [18], chemical oxidation methods [19], hydrothermal cutting strategies [20,21], and carbonizing organics routes [22–24]. Nevertheless, most reported methods require expensive equipment and complicated procedures but with low yields. Take the hydrothermal/solvothermal methods as examples, these methods have been most widely investigated for synthesis of monodisperse CDs with high crystallinity and heteroatom doping as also. Despite its appealing capacities, the hydrothermal/solvothermal process has been plagued with operating at high pressures, leading to serious safety concerns, especially when scale up synthesis [24]. Otherwise, the mechanisms of these synthesis in solvent phase are still poorly understood. Hence, it is highly important to search for a facile method for the fabrication of monodisperse CDs with high crystallinity in a controllable manner.

In view of powerful hydrogen bonds guided supramolecular chemistry for the design and self-assembly of organic molecules into complex molecular architectures, could the impart physical-chemical properties of hydrogen bonding will guide the carbonizing process during the synthesis of CDs boosts our great interest. Currently, intermolecular hydrogen bonding (NH···O) produced from the interaction between carboxylate and amino moieties has been widely exploited [25–28]. In this study, mutual, complementary NH-O hydrogen bonding between trimesic acid (TA) molecule and urea molecules is fully investigated for the direct guided heating up synthesis of monodisperse crystallized CDs in a one-step carbonization. Results demonstrates that by regulating the ratio of trimesic acid/urea, which served as assembled base blocks, the emission wavelength of the CDs will blue shift in a controllable manner. The obtained CDs demonstrated high crystallinity and uniform distribution in diameter. Furthermore, the obtained CDs could be employed for specific detection of iron ions Fe3+.

All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade. Trimesic acid, urea and diethylene glycol (DEG) were supplied by Shantou Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd., China. LiCl, NaCl, NaOH, KBr, NH4I, Na2CO3, Na2SO3, Na2S, KNO3, AgNO3, ZnCl2, NiCl2, FeCl2, CaCl2, CuSO4, MnSO4, MgSO4, Co(NO3)2, AlCl3, FeCl3 and were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Water was deionized and purified by being passed through Milli-Q-purified water of 18 M cm−1 specific resistivity.

Trimesic acid (2 mmol) and urea (3 mmol) were added into 20 mL DEG. The reaction mixture was heated up to 150 °C for 5 min with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Besides, intermolecular hydrogen bonding effects on the properties of CDs were investigated by varying the mole ratios of TA and urea from 8:3 to 1:6 according to the above procedure. The resultant solution was first centrifuged at 9000 rpm (RCF. 12,950 ×g) for 30 min to remove large particles, and then subjected to dialysis (molecular weight cut off = 1.0 kDa) to completely remove the non-reactive precursors and the DEG. The resulting CDs were stored for future characterization. The specimens were labelled according to their molar ratio of the starting materials (trimesic acid/urea) = 8/3, 4/3, 2/3, 1/3, 1/6 for CDs-83, CDs-43, CDs-23, CDs-13 and CDs-16, respectively.

All samples for detecting were keep at room temperature and a constant pH condition (pH 6.0). For Fe (III) detection procedures, 20.0 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 CDs was mixed with 980 μL water. Subsequently, 5 μL of Fe (III) ion aqueous solution with different concentrations was added to the above solution to get the final Fe (III) concentration of 0–1000 μM. The resulting solutions were incubated at room temperature under well-mixing conditions. After gently shaken for 30 s, the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded at the wavelength of 420 nm under the excitation wavelength at 340 nm. In addition, the selectivity of sensing system was conducted by adding 23 different types of 1 mM interfering ion solutions into CDs solution and then the quenching of fluorescence was recorded after gently shaking for 30 s. The measurements for the detection of metal ions were performed in triplicate.

The morphology and size of CDs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai F20) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The TEM samples were prepared by doping CDs aqueous solution onto a copper grid and dried in air. All luminescence spectra were recorded by Cray Eclipse spectrophotometer with a 10/5 nm slit width, and equipped with a 1 cm quartz cell. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a Lambda 900 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) with a 1 cm quartz cell. The excitation wavelength used was 280 nm for the emission spectra. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were carried out in a time-correlated-single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system from FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) with λex = 377 nm. The pH values of solutions were measured with a PHS-3C pH meter.

The quantum yield (QY) of the obtained CDs was measured according to the established procedure by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities and the absorbency values of CDs aqueous solution with the reference quinine sulfate (QS). The quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54 was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index of 1.33). In order to minimize re-absorption, the absorbance intensity of the samples in 1.0 cm quartz cell was keep below 0.1. QE was calculated according to Eq. (1) [29]:
(1)
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript “R” refers to reference standard with known quantum yield, here is quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intermolecular hydrogen bonding guided direct heating up synthesis of CDs and their optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs are prepared by direct heating the TA and urea self-assembly supramolecular materials in DEG at 150 °C for 5 min and then purified using a tubular dialysis membrane (Scheme 1 ). The scheme of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in the solution phase was as follows. First, the solid phase of TA and urea were dissolved, then one of the carboxylate groups in the TA molecule and amino group of the urea molecule collide via Brown motion to form two O-HN hydrogen bonds, and so forth sequentially. The possible basic structure with a symmetric two-dimensional (2D) array consisting of cyclic polygon of 6TA and 6Urea molecules in solution.

As mentioned above, TA molecules supply carboxylate O and urea molecules were deemed as H donor from amino groups to build O--H-N hydrogen bond resulting in supramolecular structure. As shown in Fig. 2 a, several other molar ratios of TA and urea (i.e., 8:3, 4:3, 2:3, 1:3, and 1:6) were applied for synthesis of the CDs. The results showed that there was blue shift in emission wavelength with increasing urea molecules content, and the color changes of CDs aqueous solution under UV light from light blue to dark blue were observed (Fig. 2a, inset). This indicates that assembly of supramolecular structure would be regulated by molar ratio as result of turning optical properties of the as-obtained CDs (Fig. S1). As the CDs exhibited relatively bright blue FL with molar ratio of 2:3, this proportioning was chosen in the synthesis of CDs.

To demonstrate the advantage of hydrogen bond guided supramolecular structure for the synthesis of high-quality CDs, the structure, composition, and optical properties of CDs-23 were fully investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the UV absorption spectra of CDs-23 aqueous solutions showed a single peak at 264 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of the conjugated CC units from the carbon core [24]. Besides, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-23 in aqueous solution were also recorded. CDs-23 exhibit an excitation dependent emission as excitation wavelength is from 300 to 370 nm with a strongest fluorescence emission band located at 432 nm when excited at 340 nm, indicating that the emission properties of CDs-23 are mostly dependent on the conjugated CC units from the carbon core. And the quantum yield is calculated to be 13.3%, and it is higher than the previously reported CDs, which might be attributed to the high crystallinity of CDs that will be discussed below [24,31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties of the obtained CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of as-prepared CDs-23 was investigated by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the obtained CDs-23 were monodisperse with a narrow size distribution. The particles size range of CDs-23 spans from 1.5 nm to 3 nm with average size of 2.3 ± 0.2 nm based on the statistical measurement results of 200 particles from TEM result. It is anticipated CDs with such narrow-distributed particle size will significantly improve the sensing performance. Besides, CDs-23 were highly crystallized with parallel fringes as confirmed by high-resolution TEM images shown in Fig. 3b. Fig. 3c shows the contrast intensity profile from the position marked with a red dashed line in Fig. 3b. The interlayer distance is about 0.21 nm, well in accordance with that of the (0 0 1) plane of graphite [33]. Furthermore, the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (Fig. 3d) also shows that the CDs-23 are almost graphene crystals with a spacing of 0.21 nm. Moreover, in the Raman spectrum (Fig. S2), the overlapping of the D-line at 1386 cm−1 and the G-line at 1580 cm−1 indicates the graphitization nature of the sample. Usually, highly crystalline CDs with high sp2 carbon dominant requires much higher temperatures (T > 200 °C) or high pressures during the synthesis process. But in this study, the highly crystalline CDs could be easily produced at low temperatures (T < 150 °C) under atmosphere, which might ascribe to the formation of hydrogen bond self-assembly supramolecular structure between TA and urea molecules that reduces the thermodynamic barrier and enhances reaction kinetics.

Due to the carboxylate and amino groups in structure of the CDs-23, the synthesized CDs-23 were inherently water-soluble. The stability was investigated by measuring FL intensity of the CDs-23 against high ionic strengths in solution, extreme pH, and strong light exposure. As shown in Fig. 4 a, even in 1 M NaCl solution, the FL intensity of the CDs-23 remained almost constant, demonstrating the outstanding stability of the CDs-23 towards highly ionic conditions. The pH effect on CDs-23 fluorescence was also studied over a wide pH range. CDs-23 fluorescence slight decreases with increasing pH (pH > 6), as was shown in Fig. 4b. The FL intensity of CDs-23 achieved maximum in the pH range from 2 to 9. Hence, the physiological condition of pH = 6 was chosen as the ideal reaction pH. Furthermore, CDs-23 exhibit excellent photostability that their FL intensity keep almost the same even after exposure of UV light illumination at 365 nm for 240 min (Fig. 4c), which could be further confirmed as the images shown in Fig. 4d that photographs of CDs-23 aqueous solution under natural light and UV light irradiation for different times with almost no changes in colors and luminosity. Taking the results above, these findings suggested outstanding stability of the CDs-23 under the ambient environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the CDs for the determination of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from its superior optical properties, CDs-23 was proved to contain many amino groups on their surface, thus imparting them the suitability for subsequent interact with analyte units. And we found CDs-23 could be selected as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective determination of Fe3+ in a label free manner. Fig. 5 a demonstrated the corresponding Fe3+ concentration-dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs-23. The FL intensity of 420 nm gradually decreased with the increasing of concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 1000 μM. The inset showed photographs of CDs-23 alone and in the presence of different Fe3+ concentration exposed to a UV lamp at 365 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency reached a maximum and kept stable when the incubation time reached 30 s. Fig. 5b showed the nonlinear Stern-Volmer quenching profile of CDs-23 with different Fe3+ ion concentration. As plotted in Fig. 5b inset, a good linear behavior was displayed between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (I0 − I)/I0 and the concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 20 μM. The linear regression equation was (I0 − I)/I0 = 0.0301C−0.0197, with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. Moreover, the detection limit was calculated to be 41.7 nM (3σ). These results demonstrate that CDs-23 can detect Fe3+ with high sensitivity.

To validate the specificity of sensing system toward Fe3+, we tested the FL intensity of the other coexisted species that may be present in water samples under an identical conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, no significant fluorescence quenching was observed for these cation interferences. For anion interferences (Fig. 6c and d), OH− and CO3 2− exhibit a moderate quenching, because of the alkaline environment in the presence of these two ions. The results revealed that CDs-23 was highly selective to Fe3+ ions, which might due to high affinity between functional groups (i. e., hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino) of CDs-23 surface and Fe3+.

The quenching mechanism was then investigated. Fig. 7 shows that the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs-23 decreased from 5.65 ns to 4.73 ns after the addition of Fe3+. All photoluminescence decays required three-component exponential fit, which implied the existence of multiple de-excitation processes. Time constants and relative amplitudes determined by the fitting were summarized in Table S1. The change of fluorescence lifetime indicated a dynamics quenching occurred. We hypothesized the plausible mechanism for the quenching of the fluorescence of CDs-23 was the intramolecular photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process from the excited CDs-23 to the Fe3+ ions (Scheme 2 ), When Fe3+ was added, complexation between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl of CDs occurred, leading to the splitting of d orbital of Fe3+. Therefore, electrons in the excited state of CDs were partially transferred to the d orbital of Fe3+ [34–37]. Electron transition in the radiation forms (FL emission) was consequently restrained, leading to fluorescence quenching, as in the case of other carbon dots. To further evaluate their practical applications, the CDs-23 were applied for detecting Fe3+ in tap water samples. As shown in Table 1 , the CDs-23 based probe for the determination of Fe3+ showed good recoveries. These results further confirmed the reliability and feasibility of the CDs-23 nanosensors for label-free fluorometric detection of Fe3+ in water.

In summary, intermolecular hydrogen bond-mediated synthesis has been proven to be an effective and facial strategy for producing highly monodisperse crystalline CDs for the first time. The synthesis of CDs could be conducted with a controllable manner by regulating and controlling the ratio of the assembled base blocks between TA and urea. Under optimization, CDs with high crystallinity and narrow size distribution can be prepared under low-temperature carbonization (<150 °C) under atmosphere, obviating high temperature or high pressure required in early reports [20–24]. Moreover, the obtained CDs could be used as fluorescent probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in real water samples. This study will provide a molecular engineering strategy for fabrication of high-quality CDs and will broaden their applications in sensing, imaging, energy devices, and so forth.
This project was supported by the the Education Bureau of Fujian Province (Project No. JAT160302 and (Project No. JA13195).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.09.050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
